project.objective: When and where did Anders Monsen marry Unna Halsteinsdatter, and what does the marriage record show?

questions.question: When and where did Anders Monsen (born 7 April 1759, Håtuft, Meland, Hordaland, Norway) marry Unna Halsteinsdatter (born 27 May 1745, Hestdal, Meland, Hordaland), and what does the marriage record show?
questions.rationale: The project objective is to locate the marriage of Anders Monsen and Unna Halsteinsdatter. Norwegian Lutheran church books (ministerialbok) covering Meland/Hamre parishes in Hordaland from the 1770s–1790s are available on Digitalarkivet and FamilySearch. Given Anders's birth in 1759 and Unna's in 1745, the marriage likely occurred between c. 1778 and 1795. The couple relationship exists in the tree but carries no marriage date, place, or record citation — this question directly addresses that gap.

exhaustive_declaration.justification: All FamilySearch-accessible sources have been systematically searched: FS Norway Marriages 1660-1926 (collection 1468080) under multiple name and place variants (logs 001-005, 008); 1801 Census at Manger (logs 006-007, positive — household confirmed); FamilySearch volume search confirmed no digitized image groups for Hamre/Meland/Osterøy parishes 1750-1800 (logs 010-011); FamilySearch full-text search returned no results. The 1801 census confirms Anders Monsen and Unna Halsteinsdatter as a co-resident couple at Manger with son Jørgen Andersen (b.~1796). No primary marriage record found in any FamilySearch source. Digitalarkivet.no holds the Hamre ministerialbok (the decisive source) but is inaccessible to this autonomous session — it requires human browsing outside the FamilySearch MCP tool scope, equivalent to a browse-only resource over the transport cap. The conclusion is accordingly limited to indirect evidence.

stop_criteria.goal_alignment: Partially met. The couple's married co-residence at Manger in 1801 is established (indirect evidence; census). The specific marriage date, place, and record content — the core of the research question — remain unestablished from currently searched sources.
stop_criteria.repository_breadth: FamilySearch indexed marriages (1468080) exhausted under all applicable name variants and place filters. FamilySearch volume search found no Hamre/Meland browse volumes for 1750-1800. FamilySearch full-text corpus contains no 18th-century Hamre parish register text. Digitalarkivet.no (the primary Norwegian parish book repository) and Ancestry are inaccessible in this autonomous session.
stop_criteria.original_substitution: The 1801 census was accessed as an FamilySearch original indexed record with image reference (src_001). The primary marriage source — the Hamre ministerialbok at Digitalarkivet.no — has not been consulted, as it is outside this session's accessible scope.
stop_criteria.independent_verification: One source document: the 1801 Norway census (src_001, original). The marriage relationship is established by this single source alone. Independent verification of the marriage event itself requires the parish marriage register, which has not been located.
stop_criteria.evidence_class: The census (src_001) is an original record. The marriage relationship evidence (a_004) is indirect — a structural inference from co-residence and child's patronymic. No primary-information direct evidence of a marriage event, date, or place has been found.
stop_criteria.conflict_resolution: The Anders Monsen birth year discrepancy (census ~1756 vs. christening 1759) is documented (a_003). It does not bear on the marriage question. No marriage-specific conflicting evidence exists among searched sources.
stop_criteria.overturn_risk: HIGH. The Hamre ministerialbok at Digitalarkivet.no for 1770-1800 is the decisive unaccessed source. It is publicly available but requires human browsing outside this session's tool scope. If consulted, it would very likely supply the exact marriage date, place, and parties — either confirming the indirect census evidence or revealing nuances (a different marriage venue, additional parties). The proof conclusion's tier is therefore limited to what indirect evidence supports. Recommended follow-up: browse Digitalarkivet.no Hamre/Meland parish marriage registers (Viede), 1770-1800, for Anders Monsen and Unna/Udna Halstensdatter/Halsteinsdatter.

items.rationale: The FamilySearch 'Norway, Marriages, 1660–1926' collection (1468080) is the largest free indexed Norwegian marriage database (1.33M records). Both Anders Monsen and Unna Halsteinsdatter were christened in Hamre parish, making this the most probable venue for their marriage c. 1778–1795. An indexed hit would supply the marriage date, place, and the record image link for verification.

Digitalarkivet.no (media.digitalarkivet.no/en/kb/browse) holds digitized images of all surviving Norwegian parish books, including the Hamre ministerialbok, with both an index and page-scan images. If the FS indexed search misses the entry (due to indexing gaps or variant spellings), the marriage register pages can be browsed directly. The original register page is also the authoritative record to read for record content — what witnesses, parental names, and ceremonial details the register shows.

The 1801 Norway Census (FS collection 3733603, index & images) is the earliest national census and would show Anders and Unna as a married couple living at Åsebø, Radøy (where Anders died in 1821). It does not give a marriage date or place, but it confirms the couple's identity, lists household members, and provides secondary evidence of the marriage — corroborating or helping identify the correct marriage record.

Ancestry hosts 'Norway, Select Marriages, 1660–1926' (collection 60095), drawn from the same underlying church-book indexes as the FamilySearch collection but with a separate search interface and sometimes wider coverage of variant name forms. Searching here independently tests whether a record missed by the FS interface surfaces under different parameters.

If indexed searches fail, the Norwegian Historical Data Centre parish register search (rhd.uit.no) and Digitalarkivet full-text search offer an unindexed page-text route to the marriage entry. Executed via the search-full-text skill using both surnames as co-occurrence terms (Monsen + Halsteinsdatter) unscoped to a specific collection, this catches entries that were missed in name-indexed transcriptions due to handwriting or variant spellings.

log.notes: 34 results returned; all match scores extremely low (max 0.018); all results show Anders Monsen as secondary party in other persons' records, not as groom. Spouse filter "Unna Halsteinsdatter" did not surface target record. Per collection-quirks, must vary given name (Unna→Urna/Udna) AND surname (Halsteinsdatter→Halsteinsdr) together.

Identical 34 results as log_001 — varying spouse given name Unna→Urna and surname Halsteinsdatter→Halsteinsdr together produced no change. The index appears to be not filtering by spouse at all for this record set. All results remain secondary-party appearances (Anders Monsen as parent/witness). Proceeding to principal-only search per collection-quirks guidance.

66 total matches; 28 titled 'Entry for Anders Monsen' (groom as principal). All scores low (max 0.044) due to sparse subject profile — no marriage facts in tree yet. Top candidates: NWZZ-WXM, NWZQ-C9Q, NWZQ-C9W. Must read individual records to identify the correct one by spouse name and place. Bride-as-principal search (Urna Halsteinsdr) returned zero.

Zero results searching bride Urna Halsteinsdr as principal in collection 1468080. Index does not surface this record under the bride's abbreviated patronymic surname. Groom-as-principal approach (log_003) is the active path.

query.notes: Hordaland-filtered principal-only search — no spouse filter; added marriagePlace filter to eliminate Gulen/Svanøy/Østfold false matches

log.notes: 52 Hordaland-filtered results. First 3 candidates read (NW4H-W8D: 1870, NW4S-ZW5: 1864, NW4V-YLW: 1852) all too late — 19th century marriages. The target marriage is c. 1778-1795. Candidates deeper in the pool may contain 18th-century entries; continuing to read from this staged set.

query.notes: 1801 Census search for Anders Monsen, expected household at Åsebø, Radøy/Manger, Hordaland

log.notes: Top match: ark:/61903/1:1:8YMV-R76Z, matchScore 0.66 (strongest candidate by far), Hamre, Hordaland, birth 1758, collection 'Norway, Census, 1801', recordArk 4H6K-B56Z — unattached to subject or other. Will read this record to confirm identity and retrieve household including Unna's indexed name.

query.notes: Refined 1801 census search — residence Manger (correct parish for Åsebø/Radøy where Anders died 1821)

log.notes: TOP MATCH: ark:/61903/1:1:8YMK-6G3Z, household at Manger, Hordaland. Household members: Anders Monsen (b. 1756), Udna Halstensdatter (b. 1745) — confirmed match for Unna Halsteinsdatter (tree: b. May 1745), Jørgen Andersen (b. 1796, patronymic = son of Anders), and Ingebor Nielsdatter (b. 1765, household head). Udna Halstensdatter born 1745 is an exact birth-year match for tree subject KZHH-VTX. Collection-quirks predicted this indexing as 'Udna Halstensdatter' — confirmed. This is indirect evidence the couple was married, with marriage terminus ante quem c. 1795 (before Jørgen b. 1796). Marriage date/place still to be found in parish books.

query.notes: Manger-specific marriage place filter — attempt to narrow to correct parish

log.notes: 52 results returned — identical pool to the Hordaland filter (pli_001 log_005). Place filter 'Manger' expands to Hordaland county; no 18th-century Manger-specific marriage entry for Anders Monsen found in the indexed collection. Indexed search for the marriage is now exhausted across all Hordaland/Manger variants. Proceeding to pli_002 (Digitalarkivet image browse) and pli_003 (1801 census extraction) as the remaining FamilySearch-accessible paths.

query.notes: Search for son Jørgen Andersen christening c.1796 as parentage anchor

log.notes: Only 1 result — Jørgen christened 21 Apr 1794, Sortland, Nordland — wrong region (far north, not Hordaland). The son Jørgen Andersen (b. 1796 per 1801 census) does not appear indexed under father Anders Monsen in Norway baptisms collection, suggesting the Manger christening register for this period is either unindexed or browsed only. No useful result.
No FamilySearch digitized image groups for Osterøy (Hamre parish) 1750–1800. Hamre parish marriage registers for this period are not available as browse-only FS volumes.

Three volumes found, all covering Haus parish (Småland, Haus, Hordaland, 1771–1773) — wrong parish. No volumes for Hamre, Meland, or Manger parishes in this period. FamilySearch has no digitized image groups for 18th-century Hamre parish marriage registers. The Hamre parish books for 1750–1800 are held at Digitalarkivet.no (Norwegian National Archives), which requires external site access outside this session.

assertions.informant_bias_notes: Pre-1940 Norwegian census; the enumerator did not record who answered for the household. The respondent was likely Anders himself or another adult household member, but this cannot be confirmed.
The enumerator personally visited the dwelling and recorded the household at this location.

Birth year stated on the 1801 census enumeration. The enumerator did not record who answered; the respondent may have been Anders himself or another adult. Note: the tree records Anders Monsen's birth as 1759 — the census gives 1756, a 3-year discrepancy possibly reflecting rounding, misremembering, or enumerator error.

The 1801 Norwegian census has no explicit relationship column. Co-residence of Anders Monsen and Udna Halstensdatter in the same household, together with a child (Jørgen Andersen b.~1796) whose patronymic indicates Anders as father, implies a spousal/conjugal relationship. The record does not state 'married' — this is a structural inference. Independently, the 1801 census predates the 1880 Norwegian census that first introduced explicit relationship columns.
The enumerator personally visited the dwelling and recorded the household at this location.
Birth year stated on the 1801 census enumeration; respondent unknown.

Pre-1940 Norwegian census; respondent unknown. Identified as Unna Halsteinsdatter (KZHH-VTX) based on birth year 1745 matching the tree and the collection-quirk prediction that she indexes as 'Udna Halstensdatter' in the 1801 census. Name variant 'Udna'/'Unna' and patronymic spelling 'Halstensdatter'/'Halsteinsdatter' are phonetically equivalent in 19th-century Norwegian records.
The enumerator personally visited the dwelling and recorded the household at this location.
Birth year stated on the 1801 census enumeration; respondent unknown. The tree records Unna Halsteinsdatter's birth as 27 May 1745 — the census-recorded year 1745 is consistent.
Pre-1940 Norwegian census; respondent unknown. The patronymic surname 'Andersen' (son of Anders) is consistent with Anders Monsen being the father.
The enumerator personally visited the dwelling and recorded the household at this location.
Birth year stated on the 1801 census; respondent unknown. A child of approximately 5 years could not self-report; the respondent was presumably a parent.

The patronymic surname 'Andersen' (son of Anders) directly implies Anders Monsen is Jørgen's father, per Norwegian patronymic naming custom. The record has no relationship column. This establishes a terminus ante quem for Anders and Unna's marriage of approximately 1795 (Jørgen b.~1796). The inference is strong given the patronymic convention but remains structural.

Inferred from household co-residence with Udna Halstensdatter (identified as Unna Halsteinsdatter, KZHH-VTX). The 1801 census has no relationship column. Udna is the only adult female in the household with Anders, and together they co-reside with Jørgen — consistent with both being his parents. This is a structural inference; the record does not state the mother's identity.

person_evidence.rationale: Name 'Anders Monsen' is an exact given-name and surname match to the research subject. Score 0.17 is degenerate (local-only tree persons without FS ARKs — not a real low-match signal). Qualitative correlation: name exact, gender Male, residence Manger 1801 consistent with death at Åsebø/Radøy (Manger parish) 1821, birth year 1756 census vs. 1759 tree within ±3 years, co-residence with Udna Halstensdatter (=Unna Halsteinsdatter KZHH-VTX, known wife). Overall match strength: Strong.
Residence Manger, Hordaland 1801 is the correct parish location for Anders Monsen (died and buried at Manger kirkegård, Åsebø, Radøy, 1821). Consistent with all known facts for LKFW-9XH.

Census-reported birth year ~1756 conflicts with tree's documented birth year 1759 (christening 7 Apr 1759). A 3-year discrepancy is within the normal range of census age-reporting inaccuracy in early 19th-century Norway. Conflict surfaced by materialize_facts; routed to conflict-resolution. Identity remains strong based on other identifiers.

Relationship assertion: Anders Monsen co-resides with Udna Halstensdatter (=KZHH-VTX, Anders's known wife) in a household where Jørgen Andersen (patronymic Andersen = son of Anders) is also present. 1801 census has no explicit relationship column; spousal relationship is inferred from household co-residence plus child's patronymic. Indirect evidence of the existing Couple relationship in the tree (rel-anders-unna).

Same relationship assertion links to both parties. Udna Halstensdatter (KZHH-VTX) is named co-resident with Anders Monsen (LKFW-9XH), with a son whose patronymic confirms Anders as father. 1801 census implies spousal relationship by household structure. Consistent with existing Couple relationship in tree.

Name 'Udna Halstensdatter' is a phonetically equivalent variant of 'Unna Halsteinsdatter' (KZHH-VTX). Collection-quirks.md explicitly predicted this indexing pattern for this woman in the 1801 census. Birth year 1745 in the census is an exact year match to the tree's May 1745. Co-residence with Anders Monsen at Manger (correct geographic area). Name variant 'Udna/Unna' and 'Halstensdatter/Halsteinsdatter' are well-documented transcription variants in Norwegian 18th/19th-century church records. Match strength: Strong.

Residence Manger, Hordaland 1801. Unna Halsteinsdatter was born in Meland/Hestdal (Hordaland) and co-resides with her husband Anders Monsen in the Manger parish — consistent with all known facts for KZHH-VTX.

Census birth year 1745 is an exact year match to the tree's known birth of May 1745 for Unna Halsteinsdatter (KZHH-VTX). This exact match, combined with the name variant Udna/Unna, constitutes strong corroboration for the identity.

New person. Jørgen Andersen is materialized as stub I1 from this census record. Name 'Jørgen' (given) + 'Andersen' (patronymic = son of Anders) consistent with being a son of Anders Monsen (LKFW-9XH). Born approximately 1796, residing at Manger, Hordaland. No prior tree entry; created via materialize_facts.
Residence Manger, Hordaland 1801 for Jørgen Andersen (I1), consistent with the household of his parents Anders Monsen and Unna Halsteinsdatter.
Birth ~1796, census-reported for Jørgen Andersen (I1). A child of ~5 years in 1801; parents likely reported this. Establishes marriage terminus ante quem for Anders and Unna: married by c.1795.

Patronymic 'Andersen' (son of Anders) + household co-residence with Anders Monsen (LKFW-9XH) establishes Jørgen as Anders's child. Relationship inferred from Norwegian patronymic naming custom; 1801 census has no relationship column.

Same assertion bears on LKFW-9XH as the father: Jørgen's patronymic 'Andersen' names Anders Monsen as father. Indirect evidence of parentage per Norwegian naming convention; 1801 census structural inference.

Household co-residence with Udna Halstensdatter (=KZHH-VTX) as the adult female in the household. 1801 census has no explicit relationship column; Udna is inferred as Jørgen's mother from household structure.

Same assertion bears on KZHH-VTX (Unna Halsteinsdatter) as the mother. Udna Halstensdatter co-resides with Anders Monsen and their son Jørgen; she is inferred as Jørgen's mother by household structure and the absence of any other adult female as co-parent candidate.

Assertion records name 'Ingebor Nielsdatter' for the household_member persona p_260262119300 in the 1801 Manger census. Ingebor is a new stub person created from this record. Name matches exactly; her birth year (~1765) and residence (Manger 1801) are consistent with a co-resident of Anders Monsen's household. No competing candidates.
Residence at Manger 1801 is consistent with the household membership of Ingebor Nielsdatter (I2), the same person evidenced by a_005 in this household record.

Birth ~1765 is an approximate census-derived figure for Ingebor Nielsdatter (I2). Consistent with her being a younger household member (born ~20 years after Unna Halsteinsdatter). Census ages are approximate; the value is treated as probable rather than confident.

proof_summaries.exhaustive_search_summary: FamilySearch 'Norway, Marriages, 1660–1926' (collection 1468080) was searched under nine strategies covering spouse-filtered, principal-only, and place-filtered queries (logs 001–005, 008–009); no 18th-century Hordaland marriage entry for Anders Monsen was identified. FamilySearch volume search for Osterøy/Meland/Hordaland 1750–1800 confirmed no digitized image groups for Hamre or Meland parish registers (logs 010–011). The FamilySearch full-text corpus yielded no results for this period and parish. The 1801 Norway Census at Manger (log 007) confirmed the household. Digitalarkivet.no (primary repository for surviving Norwegian parish books, including the Hamre ministerialbok) and Ancestry were not accessed — both are publicly available but require human browsing outside this session's FamilySearch MCP tool scope. Overturn risk is HIGH: the Hamre parish marriage register for 1770–1800 at Digitalarkivet.no is the decisive unaccessed source.
proof_summaries.narrative_markdown:
## The Marriage of Anders Monsen and Unna Halsteinsdatter: A Proof Argument

**Research Question:** When and where did Anders Monsen (born 7 April 1759, Håtuft, Meland, Hordaland, Norway; died 1821, Åsebø, Radøy, Hordaland) marry Unna Halsteinsdatter (born 27 May 1745, Hestdal, Meland, Hordaland), and what does the marriage record show?

### Sources Searched

The FamilySearch "Norway, Marriages, 1660–1926" collection (collection 1468080, approximately 1.33 million indexed records) was searched under nine strategies: groom-as-principal with spouse variants (Unna / Urna / Udna Halsteinsdatter / Halsteinsdr), principal-only searches filtered by birth year, and Hordaland- and Manger-filtered place searches. No 18th-century Hordaland marriage entry for an Anders Monsen marrying a woman corresponding to Unna Halsteinsdatter was identified in the indexed collection. FamilySearch's volume catalog, searched for digitized image groups covering Osterøy/Hamre and Meland/Manger parishes for 1750–1800, returned only three volumes, all covering Haus parish — not the target parish. FamilySearch's full-text corpus yielded no results for this period and location.

The Norway Census, 1801 (FamilySearch collection 3733603) was searched and returned the confirmed household at Manger, Hordaland. The Hamre ministerialbok (Lutheran parish marriage register) for 1770–1800, held at Digitalarkivet.no (the Norwegian National Archives digital portal), was not consulted in this research session — it is the decisive source for this question type and requires human browsing outside this session's tool scope.

### Evidence from the 1801 Norway Census

"Norges folketelling, 1801," household of Ingebor Nielsdatter, Manger, Hordaland ("Norway, Census, 1801," FamilySearch ark:/61903/1:2:4HXX-9H6Z; original government enumeration, 1801) is an **original record** containing **indeterminate information** for household-reported name and age data, providing **indirect evidence** of the marital relationship. The enumeration lists four persons:

1. **Anders Monsen**, head of household, born approximately 1756 (census-reported).
2. **Ingebor Nielsdatter**, born approximately 1765.
3. **Udna Halstensdatter**, born approximately 1745.
4. **Jørgen Andersen**, born approximately 1796.

**Identity of Anders Monsen (LKFW-9XH):** The census subject's given name, surname, location (Manger, the parish of his 1821 death at neighboring Åsebø, Radøy), and birth year (~1756, within three years of his confirmed christening in 1759) are consistent with the research subject. No other Anders Monsen household in Manger, Hordaland is present in the indexed results.

**Identity of Udna Halstensdatter as Unna Halsteinsdatter (KZHH-VTX):** The 1801 Norwegian census indexes Unna Halsteinsdatter as "Udna Halstensdatter" — a collection-specific phonetic transcription documented in collection-quirks research. Her birth year ~1745 in the census is an exact match with Unna Halsteinsdatter's confirmed christening date of 27 May 1745. No alternative candidate among the census results matches on birth year and location.

**Evidence of the marital relationship:** The 1801 Norwegian census carried no explicit relationship or marital-status column (such columns were not introduced until the 1880 census). The conjugal relationship between Anders Monsen and Udna Halstensdatter is a structural inference: they appear as co-resident adults in the same household, and Jørgen Andersen (born ~1796) bears the patronymic "Andersen" — the standard Norwegian derivative of the father's given name "Anders." A child named Andersen had a father named Anders. Co-residence of two adults with a child bearing the male adult's patronymic is the characteristic census signature of a married couple and their child in this period and culture.

### Analysis

The 1801 census is a single original source. For the marriage relationship, it provides only indirect evidence — no record statement exists. The conclusion therefore rests on one source document of one institutional provenance: it constitutes a single evidence unit regardless of the number of corroborating details within it. Independent verification from a second source (such as the marriage register itself, a probate record, or a later census entry with an explicit relationship column) has not been obtained.

The Anders Monsen birth year discrepancy — census ~1756 versus christening record 1759 — is characteristic of census age approximation and does not affect the identity conclusion; census ages in this period regularly differed from true birth years by one to several years, and the two values bracket a plausible birth around 1757–1759.

### Conclusion

The preponderance of available evidence strongly suggests that Anders Monsen (LKFW-9XH) and Unna Halsteinsdatter (KZHH-VTX) were married. Their co-residence at Manger, Hordaland, in 1801, together with a son whose patronymic identifies Anders as his father, is the characteristic census profile of a married couple in 18th–19th-century Norway. The conclusion is drawn at the **probable** tier, reflecting one original source with indirect evidence and no independent verification.

The research question asks specifically when and where they married and what the marriage record shows. These specifics cannot be answered from the sources consulted. The evidence establishes only a terminus ante quem of approximately 1795 (nine months before Jørgen's birth ~1796) and that the marriage likely occurred in the Hamre or Meland parish area where both parties were christened and later resided.

The Hamre ministerialbok for 1770–1800 at Digitalarkivet.no is the primary source that would answer this question. **Recommended follow-up:** Browse Digitalarkivet.no (https://www.digitalarkivet.no) for Hamre and Meland parish marriage registers (*Viede* / *Kopulerte*), 1770–1800, under Anders Monsen and Unna / Udna Halsteinsdatter / Halstensdatter. Ancestry's "Norway, Select Marriages, 1660–1926" is a secondary fallback. A successful search would advance this conclusion to proved.
